metadata.title: Research RAS Undruggability: Why Did It Take 40 Years to Drug the Most Common Oncogene?

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: So the whole thing hinges on G12C accidentally creating a reactive cysteine that sotorasib can covalently grab in the GDP-bound state, while G12D swaps in aspartate and offers no such handle. I want to see how far that chemistry logic stretches. Help me: (1) map each common KRAS variant to whether it presents any covalent-able residue and rank them by druggability, (2) explain how MRTX1133 achieves affinity through non-covalent switch-II binding without a cysteine, and (3) sketch what a G12V-specific strategy would even need chemically.

props.command: So the whole thing hinges on G12C accidentally creating a reactive cysteine that sotorasib can covalently grab in the GDP-bound state, while G12D swaps in aspartate and offers no such handle. I want to see how far that chemistry logic stretches. Help me: (1) map each common KRAS variant to whether it presents any covalent-able residue and rank them by druggability, (2) explain how MRTX1133 achieves affinity through non-covalent switch-II binding without a cysteine, and (3) sketch what a G12V-specific strategy would even need chemically.
props.segment: Research RAS Undruggability: Why Did It

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. KRAS — forty years, one accident.

KRAS is mutated in thirty percent of all human cancers and nearly ninety percent of pancreatic cancers. For forty years it was undruggable. Sotorasib appeared in 2021. The question: why did one specific mutation finally yield — and why does G12D, the most common variant in pancreatic cancer, still not have an approved drug?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- problem card: KRAS prevalence; 40-year undruggable period; G12C vs G12D gap

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to research the complete history of KRAS-targeted therapy.

props.command:
claude "Research the history and current status of
KRAS-targeted therapy. What structural features
made it undruggable for 40 years? What chemical
insight enabled the G12C covalent inhibitors
sotorasib and adagrasib? What are their clinical
response rates from CodeBreaK 100 and KRYSTAL-1?
What is the clinical development status of
KRAS G12D inhibitors?"

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) -- Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code. The script builds the KRAS inhibitor comparison — variant, frequency in NSCLC and pancreatic cancer, approved drug, ORR, and the chemical mechanism that makes the variant druggable or not.

props.code:
# kras_status.py -- Claude Code output
VARIANTS = [
  {"variant": "G12C",
   "nsclc_freq": "13%", "pdac_freq": "1%",
   "drug": "sotorasib / adagrasib",
   "orr": "37% / 43%",
   "mech": "covalent to reactive cysteine",
   "status": "FDA APPROVED"},
  {"variant": "G12D",
   "nsclc_freq": "4%", "pdac_freq": "41%",
   "drug": "MRTX1133 (trial)",
   "orr": "pending Phase 2",
   "mech": "non-covalent; no reactive handle",
   "status": "Phase 2"},
  {"variant": "G12V",
   "nsclc_freq": "8%", "pdac_freq": "34%",
   "drug": "none approved",
   "orr": "N/A",
   "mech": "no selective approach yet",
   "status": "Preclinical"},
]
for v in VARIANTS:
  print(f"{v['variant']}: {v['status']} | {v['drug']} | ORR {v['orr']}")
  print(f"  Chem: {v['mech']}")

beats.narration_text: The timeline. 1982: KRAS discovered as a viral oncogene homolog. Forty years of undruggable period. 2021: sotorasib approved for G12C NSCLC, ORR thirty-seven percent. 2022: adagrasib approved, ORR forty-three percent. G12D: MRTX1133 in Phase 2, no ORR data yet. G12V: no approved drug, preclinical.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- Remotion timeline to be built: events on horizontal axis from 1982 to present

beats.narration_text: Now ask the specific chemistry question: what makes G12D harder than G12C?

props.command:
claude "What is the key difference in chemical strategy
required to target G12D versus G12C? The reactive
cysteine approach won't work for G12D -- what
approaches are in trials, and have any phase 2
data been reported?"

beats.narration_text: The chemistry contrast. G12C has a reactive cysteine in the switch-II pocket. Sotorasib forms a covalent bond to that cysteine when KRAS is in the GDP-bound state. G12D replaces glycine with aspartate — no reactive cysteine, no covalent handle. MRTX1133 uses non-covalent binding to the switch-II pocket. Phase 2 results are pending.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- Remotion timeline to be built: G12D programs added to the axis with trial phase markers

beats.narration_text: The reactive cysteine was an accident of the specific mutation. The G12C drug works because one nucleotide change created a unique chemical handle that G12D lacks. Selectivity came from chemistry meeting mutation — not from choosing the most biologically important variant. G12D is more common but harder.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: accident of mutation; chemistry meeting nucleotide change

beats.narration_text: If your cancer type carries a KRAS mutation, determine the specific variant — G12C, G12D, or G12V — before asking about targeted therapy. Only G12C has an approved inhibitor. Test first; the variant determines the options.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: variant-specific testing; G12C only has approved drug

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The reactive cysteine was an accident of the specific mutation. The G12C drug works because one nucleotide change created a unique chemical handle that G12D lacks. Selectivity came from chemistry meeting mutation — not from choosing the most biologically important variant. G12D is more common but harder. If your cancer type carries a KRAS mutation, determine the specific variant — G12C, G12D, or G12V — before asking about targeted therapy. Only G12C has an approved inhibitor. Test first; the variant determines the options.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research RAS Undruggability: Why Did It Take 40 Years to Drug the Most] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
